title: Types of Models — ODEs, PDEs, SDEs, and Master Equations

lines.text: Section three. Types of mathematical models you will encounter.
Not all biological models are the same.
The mathematical machinery varies enormously depending on what you are trying to capture, and the textbook walks through the main classes.
At the very finest scale, there is molecular dynamics — atom-by-atom simulation of protein motions, ligand binding, conformational changes.
This is the highest-resolution modelling available.
Most of the time, it is impractical for synthetic biology design.
Molecular dynamics is computationally expensive and requires parameter values that may not be known accurately.

And the questions a synthetic biologist usually asks — what is the steady-state protein level, how does the output oscillate, what is the response time to an input — are not best answered at the atomic level.
So coarse-grained models are the workhorses.
Four main types appear repeatedly.
First: ordinary differential equations, abbreviated ODEs.
An ODE expresses the rate of change of a variable as a function of time, with the variable depending only on time and not on space.
Each species — mRNA concentration, protein concentration, signalling molecule concentration — gets one variable, and the system is described by a set of coupled ODEs.
ODEs implicitly assume spatial homogeneity: every molecule of a given species behaves the same, regardless of where in the cell it is.
For many synthetic biology systems this is a fine approximation, and ODEs are the most commonly used framework.
The original toggle switch by Gardner, Cantor, and Collins, and the original repressilator by Elowitz and Leibler, were both modelled with ODEs.
Second: partial differential equations, PDEs.
A PDE allows variables to depend not just on time but also on space.
When spatial variation matters — when concentrations differ across regions of a cell or across regions of a colony — PDEs are the appropriate tool.

Reaction-diffusion equations are the canonical example: a concentration at each point in space changes over time according to both reactions happening at that point and diffusion of the species from neighbouring points.
PDEs were used to capture the spatial dynamics of synchronised bacterial oscillators distributed across a culture.
PDEs are mathematically harder than ODEs and computationally more expensive, but for spatially-resolved synthetic biology designs they are essential.
Third: stochastic differential equations, SDEs.
ODEs and PDEs are deterministic — given the same initial conditions, you always get the same trajectory.
But biology is noisy.

When the number of molecules of a species is small — say, a few tens of transcription factor molecules — the discrete random events of biochemistry start to matter, and the smooth continuous deterministic description fails to capture the actual behaviour.
SDEs add a stochastic noise term to differential equations to capture this random variation, using the Langevin approximation or similar techniques.
Variables still take continuous values, but their trajectories now include random fluctuations.
SDE simulations from the same initial conditions give different trajectories each run, and you average over many runs to get statistical properties.
Fourth: chemical master equations, CMEs.
These are the most rigorous treatment of stochastic biochemistry.
Rather than treating concentrations as continuous variables with random fluctuations, CMEs treat molecule counts as discrete integer values that change in discrete jumps when reactions fire.
The CME describes the time evolution of the joint probability distribution over all possible molecule-count states.
Mathematically beautiful; computationally expensive for systems with many species or many molecules.
Simulating CMEs is typically done with the Gillespie algorithm, which generates individual sample paths through the discrete state space.
Comparison: deterministic frameworks like ODEs are fast and capture average behaviour; stochastic frameworks like SDEs and CMEs capture noise but cost more compute.
Hybrid frameworks that combine deterministic and stochastic descriptions within the same model are an active research area, used when some species have high copy numbers and others have low.
And the textbook offers a practical workflow tip.
Start with deterministic models — ODEs are simpler and faster to develop.
If the deterministic design behaves as desired, test whether stochastic effects would compromise it by building the corresponding stochastic model.
If they would, refine the design until stochastic effects no longer dominate.
Only design specifically for stochastic phenomena when stochasticity is the feature, not the bug — for example, when you want population-level diversification or noise-driven decision making.
